# Transcrição: 08-00 - The Story

- **Fonte:** 08-00 - The Story
- **Duração:** 13:08
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right, it's time to tell my story. This is going to be the first draft of this story. I've never actually spoken it in front of an audience before. And so it's likely to change, because that's what happens with stories too. As we stand in front of people and we watch them react and we hear their response, the story may change. But this is my first draft of the story. I'm pretty happy with it. I think that when I take it to a stage, it's probably a close approximation to what it will ultimately become. I hope you enjoy it. I'm standing in a McDonald's play place, which amounts to an enormous Petri dish, filled with some of the most vile germs known to man. It's a plastic container filled with the detritus of kids who have occupied this space before me. And I am here standing below this tube, this tube about 12 feet over my head, yellow and red. And inside that tube, there's a little boy. He's giggling. I can see him crawling across. He's just crossed this cargo net that sort of separates the two tubes and he's crawling to the end. I know this boy. I know him well. His name is Charlie and he is my son. To my right on the ground is my daughter Clara. She's sitting at a table. The table is probably covered in flesh-eating bacteria, but I don't care because it's breakfast time and she's happy. She's eating pancakes. She loves pancakes. But that doesn't really mean that the pancakes are objectively good. Clara only eats five food items. And so when you only like five food items, all five are sort of perceived as wonderful because when your palate is so limited, you gotta love the things that you love because there's so few of them. So she's stuffing her mouth with pancakes. She's not going into the tubes today. She does not wanna go in the tubes today. She sees the tower and the tubes and even the ball pit. All of it is too much for her. She cannot handle the color and the vibrancy. Everything about it bothers her. And this doesn't surprise me. This is who Clara is. When Clara was two years old, we were sitting on the couch watching Curious George. It was Curious George's birthday. There was a birthday cake on the end of the table. And as the table wobbled, the birthday cake started to shake. Percuriously, it might fall off the table. And so Clara, age two, ran from the room. Clara couldn't handle the threat of a cartoon cake falling onto a cartoon floor. And a cartoon that featured a monkey and a man in a yellow hat occupying space in New York City. It was just too much for her. When Clara was four years old in preschool, she participated in the pot and pan parade in honor of Johnny Appleseed. The kids read about Johnny Appleseed. They planted the little apple trees. And then they took pots and pans and put them on their heads and marched up and down the hallway while the parents stood on the edges and took pictures.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

One child wept that day. One child cried inconsolably. It was Clara. This is who Clara is. Clara does not like change. She does not like challenge. She does not like things that are different or odd or unusual. She prefers ground level with pancakes. Charlie, on the other hand, is completely the opposite. Charlie has never met an object in his entire life that he hasn't wanted to taste. So he puts everything into his mouth no matter what it is. Charlie is a kid who's never met a bed that he doesn't wanna jump on or a couch that he doesn't wanna jump off. He is a daredevil. So I am not surprised that he's 12 feet off the ground in one of these tubes, except now I am surprised because I hear him crying. So I look up and I see Charlie. He's on the edge of that cargo net. He needs to cross the net to get to the other side and he looks stuck. He's crying and I say, what's wrong, Charlie? And he says, I can't cross the net. And I laugh because he just crossed it like two seconds ago. Typical kid, I say, Charlie, you just wanna cross the net, just go back. And he says, I can't. I roll my eyes, ridiculous. I say, Charlie, go across the net. He can't. He begins to cry more. And now it starts to sort of hit me in my heart. It's a ridiculous situation, but when my son cries, it sort of kills me. And so I get a little angry with him. I'm like, cross the net. In other words, stop making me feel bad. Get across the net. And he still won't cross the net. I can't go up to help him. There's a sign right on the play place that says not fit for adults. I am a grown ass man. There's no way I can go up there. Climbing the tower itself will almost be impossible for me. It's too narrow. But once I get to that tube, there's no way that tube is gonna support my weight. Get across the net, Charlie, and still he won't go. I don't know what to do. I could call my wife. It's a Sunday morning. I could wake her up. I could say, honey, it's time to get out of bed. It's time to drive down to McDonald's because Charlie's stuck in a tube. And I don't know how to get him out. Could you please come and help me? I'm not gonna do that. I'm not crazy. And so I turned to Clara. Clara, I say, can you please go save your brother? Clara takes a pancake and stuffs it in her mouth as if this will cause me to look away. She wants nothing to do with this or she's gonna continue to eat pancakes and forget her father ever asked her anything. I look back up, Charlie is really weeping now. He's stuck and he doesn't think he's ever gonna get out. I know he'll eventually get out, but for a kid, eventually could be hours from now. He might be up there for days if I don't do something. I turn back to Clara. Clara, your brother is stuck in the tube. He needs your help. Please, can you go help him? Clara doesn't say a word. She stands up, she walks across the play place to the tower and she looks up, sort of eyeing it. I don't expect her to climb it. Looking at a tower and actually climbing a tower

## [06:03 - 09:03]

are two very different things. The tower is a sort of series of platforms that she's gonna have to climb up all the way the 12 feet to get to the tower and I don't see it happening, except now I see it happening. Clara's climbing up the tower, platform to platform to platform all the way to the top. She gets to the tube. She's got about eight feet of tube to get across before she gets to the cargo net and she looks at me through one of those little holes and she says, I'm afraid dad. And I say, I know, but go help your brother, please. And she does. She crawls through that tube that she's terrified of until she gets to the cargo net. And now I can see my kids, my son on one side, my daughter on the other, separated by about three feet of net. I say, Clara, cross the net and get him. And she says, I can't. I say, Charlie, cross the net to your sister. He says, I can't, he's still crying. Now I've managed to get two kids stuck in a tube. I don't know what to do. Clara, please go get your brother. And then I watch and I can't believe what happens next. Clara reaches her hand across the net and she whispers to Charlie. This is what she says. Charlie, whisper the name of the person you love the most and that will give you courage. It'll give you the courage to cross the net. I can't believe it. This is not something I have ever told Clara. I know this is not something Alicia, my wife, has ever told Clara. She certainly didn't see it on Curious George. I don't think she's heard it anywhere in her life. This is a thing that Clara has come up with on her own. Genuine, authentic wisdom. A strategy that she has apparently been using to move through life. I have spent my life wondering how my frightened little daughter, who is nervous that the world is too high and too narrow and too loud and too intense, I've spent my life worried about how she'll navigate this world as she grows older. I've genuinely worried about how my daughter would navigate the world when I wasn't standing beside her anymore to help her. And in that instant, I'm a lot less worried. She's a very little girl who has found a very remarkable way of finding courage in a world that she finds very scary. And it works. Charlie whispers something. I have no idea what he whispers. I'd like to think it's my name, but I think it's probably my wife's name.

## [09:04 - 12:05]

Whatever he whispers, it works. He whispers something and then he reaches across to Clara and in the middle of that cargo net, their hands clasp. And then he comes across. I cannot believe what I am witnessing. I'm looking at two kids. One who was afraid of nothing in this world. Who I don't worry about at all, but probably should. And another one who seems to be terrified of everything in this world, who I worry about a lot, but maybe I shouldn't. I've got these two amazing children. And it turns out that someday, when I am no longer walking beside them, I think they're gonna be able to walk on their own and find their own way, navigate this world together or on their own, but either way, I think they're gonna be able to do it. I think my kids are gonna be okay. Thank you. As I hear their reactions, as I see their expressions, as I watch their eyes widen at spots that I want them to widen and maybe widen at spots I don't want them to widen, I might start to change my story. Think about your story as a living being. As a thing that's going to continue to change until you reach the point that you know it's right, that you know that the story you're telling is as close as possible to the thing

## [12:05 - 13:08]

that you're trying to say. Then record it. You'll have it forever. You'll have this glorious, wonderful recording of the story. And the wonderful thing about recording is this. 10 years from now, if I wanna tell this story again, all I'll have to do is listen to the recording of my story a few times and it will come right back to me. It'll happen for you too, I promise. Once you tell your story enough, it's going to live inside you. Now you might need to dust it off. You might need to sort of, you know, pull up the weeds and clear off the cobwebs to tell it, but a recording of the story just a few times will do all of that work for you and you'll be ready to tell it again. So when you're happy with it, make a recording and then tell it to people. Tell it to lots and lots and lots of people. Telling stories is a wonderful thing. It will bring you closer to people. It'll get people to believe in you. It'll get people to feel better about their own lives. So go tell that story. Good luck.
